title: Missing Receipts
story:
A support worker finishes a shift at 10pm. They bought groceries at 6pm, shoved the receipt in their pocket, drove home, put their uniform in the wash. Receipt is gone. They don't think about it again.

Finance sends a reminder to an email they check twice a week. They see it, mean to deal with it, forget.

Now finance has a transaction with no receipt. They can't code it. They can't close the month. So they chase. And chase. And chase.

Multiply by 50 staff. That is their entire job at month-end. Not analysis, not strategy — chasing humans for pieces of paper.

And even when the receipt arrives three weeks late — they still have to manually match, check coding, fix it, push it through. Every. Single. One.
phoneQ: “When a receipt doesn’t come in, whose job is it to chase it? And what happens if they never get it?”
rootCause: People spending money are not finance people. The system relies on them doing the right thing after a long shift. They won't.

title: Wrong GL Coding
story:
A support worker at the end of a 10 hour shift. They open the expense app. There's a dropdown with 200 cost centres. They have no idea what half mean. They pick the closest one. Or the one they always pick. Or leave it blank.

Finance gets that expense. The coding is wrong. They have to work out what it should have been — which location, which funding source, which program. Fix it manually. Push it through.

If they don't catch it — and sometimes they don't — it flows through to the accounting system wrong. Reporting is wrong. Funding allocation is wrong. A participant's budget gets charged incorrectly. In an NDIS org, that's not just an admin problem — that's a compliance problem.

This isn't because workers are careless. It's because you're asking a carer to make an accounting decision at the end of a long shift. That will never work.
phoneQ: “When a staff member submits an expense, what information do they need to provide? And how often does it come through accurately the first time?”
rootCause: You cannot rely on non-finance people to make accurate accounting decisions under fatigue. The system design is broken.

title: Shared Cards
theySay: We've got a card for each house.
story:
One card. Four staff members using it across the week. A transaction comes through on Thursday for $340 at a hardware store. Finance looks at it — what was this for? Which staff member? Was it authorised?

There is no way to know. The card doesn't say who used it. The bank statement doesn't either. Unless someone wrote it in a logbook — which they didn't — that transaction is a mystery.

Now imagine an auditor questions that $340. Finance has to prove it was legitimate, what it was for, who authorised it, which participant it relates to. They can't.

This is not just an admin problem. It is an audit exposure problem. A fraud risk problem. Anyone with access to that card can spend anything and finance has no way to pin it to a person.
phoneQ: “When you look at a transaction on your card statement, can you tell me which staff member made that purchase?”
rootCause: Shared cards destroy accountability and audit trails. There is no way to attribute spend to an individual when multiple people share one card.

title: Broken Approvals
theySay: Our approval process could be better.
story:
First, the thing most people miss: all of this is post-spend. By the time an approval happens, the money has already left the account. The purchase is done. Finance is doing forensic work on something that already happened.

That means approvals work completely differently depending on how the spend happened. Two scenarios:

Scenario A — Corporate card. Staff member taps the card, money is gone instantly. An approval afterwards cannot reverse that spend. Declining it in the system doesn't get the money back. What it does is create a formal record that the spend wasn't authorised — the paper trail for disciplinary action. It's a compliance and audit tool, not a financial control. The real control on cards is pre-spend: spend limits, merchant restrictions, individual cards so spend is attributed to a person.

Scenario B — Expense reimbursement. Staff member paid out of their own pocket and is claiming it back. Here approvals have actual teeth — decline it and the company's money never moves. That's a real control.

Most orgs don't distinguish between these two. They run one approval process for everything, managed by one person who goes on leave, rubber-stamps at volume, or has no real context for whether the spend was legitimate.

The question that exposes it: "When your approver clicks approve — what are they actually able to verify in that moment?"
phoneQ: “Walk me through what happens after a staff member makes a purchase — is that usually a card spend or a reimbursement? And who approves it before it hits your books?”
rootCause: All approvals are post-spend — the money is already gone. On cards they are an audit trail, not a financial control. On reimbursements they have real teeth. Most orgs don't know the difference, and their one-size-fits-all process is theatrical either way.

title: Month-End Chaos
story:
Every single pain — missing receipts, wrong coding, missing approvals, unidentified transactions — all lands at once in the last week of the month.

Finance is simultaneously chasing receipts from three weeks ago, correcting codes, getting approvals signed off, matching transactions to statements, fixing everything unresolved, and pushing clean data into the accounting system.

Some receipts will never come. Some staff have left. Some transactions get closed with "receipt unavailable" — a compliance risk waiting for an auditor to find.

By the time it's done — genuinely done — it's the 14th or 15th of the following month. Halfway through November before they know what happened in October.

The CFO sees the report arrive late and thinks finance is slow. They don't see the 180 hours of manual work. Finance wears the blame for a problem that starts the moment a support worker forgets to take a receipt out of their pocket.
phoneQ: “What does the last week of the month look like for your finance team? And what date do you typically have a clean view of last month’s spend?”
rootCause: Month-end is a tax on every broken process above it. The later the clean report, the more invisible the finance team's effort — and the deeper the chaos.
